paragraphs.en: This summer, every trendy dessert joint has a mile-long line of transplants and tourists. One New Yorker is protesting in his own way—by “baa”-ing at them.
paragraphs.zh: 这个夏天，每家潮流甜品店外都有一条几乎能走好长一英里的队伍，全是新移民和游客。一个纽约人正在用自己的方式抗议——对着他们“咩咩”叫。

paragraphs.en: It was 5:55 p.m. on a Thursday, nearly time for supper, an hour when no one has any business getting themselves a sweet treat. Yet fifty-odd people were standing on line along an aesthetically grim stretch of Seventh Avenue, waiting to purchase cups of frozen yogurt from the new West Village location of Myka, a Madrid-based dessert chain.
paragraphs.zh: 那是星期四傍晚5点55分，快到晚饭时间了，人们通常这会儿都不该去买甜点。可在曼哈顿第七大道一段看起来有些阴郁的地方，仍有五十多人排起了队，等着到位于西村的新分店 Myka（总部在马德里）的店里买装着冷冻酸奶的杯子。

paragraphs.en: One witness, a young man in a white T-shirt and athletic shorts, with three-day stubble, wasn’t having it. Walking alongside the queue, he shook his head in disbelief. “Baa!” he shouted at the idling fro-yo pilgrims. “Baa! Baa!”
paragraphs.zh: 一位目击者——一位穿白色 T 恤和运动短裤、留着三天胡茬的年轻人——对此表示不以为然。他沿着队伍走着，不敢相信地摇了摇头。 “咩！” 他对那些正在停着不动、排着队的冷冻酸奶朝圣者们喊道。 “咩！咩！”

paragraphs.en: The young man was Ezra (he prefers not to reveal his last name), a twenty-seven-year-old native New Yorker, one of five children raised in South Brooklyn by parents he describes affectionately as “modern hippies.” He is the brains behind the Instagram account @sheepofnyc, which, since its début, in May, has attracted more than seventy-five hundred followers.
paragraphs.zh: 这位年轻人叫 Ezra（他不愿透露姓氏），27 岁，地道的纽约人，五个孩子之一，由他亲切地称为“现代嬉皮士”的父母在纽约南布鲁克林抚养长大。他是 Instagram 账号 @sheepofnyc 的幕后创始人。该账号自今年 5 月上线以来，已吸引了七千五百多名关注者。

paragraphs.en: As virality goes, this is not a big number, but the passion that the account has aroused dwarfs its reach. Some commenters on Ezra’s posts view his feed as a referendum on who is and isn’t a real New Yorker. Anyone who waits on line for fro-yo must be a “transplant” who needs to “move back to Ohio,” one person wrote. A @sheepofnyc clip of a long line outside Caffe Paradiso, on Elizabeth Street, home of the social-media-vaunted Salted Brown Butter and Oat Latte, has engendered such comments as “This is a microcosm of everything wrong in current culture” and “In Spanish, we say, ‘Pendejos.’ ”
paragraphs.zh: 如果按“爆红”规模来算，这个数字不算大，但这个账号引发的热情远超它的传播范围。Ezra 的一些评论者把他的动态当成了一场“谁是真正纽约人、谁不是”的全民投票。一位网友写道：凡是在奶昔（冷冻酸奶）店排队的人都应该是“外地移民”，应当“回俄亥俄州去”。而 @sheepofnyc 曾发布的一段视频，拍的是伊丽莎白街著名的 Caffe Paradiso 门口长长的队伍——那里是社交媒体盛赞的 Salted Brown Butter 和 Oat Latte 的发源地——就引发了这样的留言： “这是当前文化中一切问题的缩影。” 以及 “在西班牙语里我们会说，‘Pendejos。’ ”

paragraphs.en: Ezra himself prefers a more minimalist approach to offering commentary on the latte line, or any line. In the clip, he is heard saying, “Baa! Baa! Baa! Baa! Look at all these sheep. I’m just a shepherd tryin’ to herd the flock... for coffee.”
paragraphs.zh: Ezra 对在拿铁店排队这件事，或任何排队现象，自己更倾向于用一种极简方式来表达观点。在这段视频里，他说：“咩！咩！咩！咩！看，这么多羊。

paragraphs.en: Long-line madness is not new to New York. If your memory goes as far back as 2013, the era of the Cronut bubble, you will recall the hour-long waits in SoHo outside Dominique Ansel Bakery. But this spring’s fresh bloom of lines has broken locals’ brains. The Village is home to not only Myka but a new outpost of the fro-yo destination Mimi’s, on University Place. After an arduously long winter, these narrow, modestly sized shops have been—with a suddenness and intensity that only TikTok and Instagram can foment—thronged. Most of the line-waiters are young. Many of them post trophy pics of their quarry; Myka offers a particularly photogenic swirl of vanilla yogurt topped with green pistachio sauce and toasty crumbled baklava.
paragraphs.zh: “排长队”式的狂热在纽约并不新鲜。如果你的记忆能追溯到2013年——也就是“克鲁诺特（Cronut）”热潮的年代，你就会想起索霍（SoHo）外侧香浓的 Dominique Ansel Bakery 门口那种足足一小时的排队。可是今年春天这种新一轮挤爆式排队又把本地人给搞懵了。这个村里（East Village 一带）不仅有 Myka，还有位于 University Place 的新店 Mimi’s，这家酸奶冰淇淋店也成了打卡圣地。经历了漫长艰难的冬天之后，这些狭窄、体量不大的店铺突然像被点燃一样爆满，而这种突然且猛烈的涌入只能归功于 TikTok 和 Instagram。排队者大多是年轻人。许多人会把自己“猎获”的战果发成胜利照；其中 Myka 的招牌是极具观赏性的卷状原味酸奶，上面淋着绿松子酱并撒着酥脆碎巴基拉瓦（baklava）。

paragraphs.en: It’s this aspect of the line phenomenon—that the behavior is driven by algorithms and the influencer economy—that gets under Ezra’s skin. He pronounced himself more baffled than angered. “It started as a joke,” he said. “It blew my mind seeing all these lines everywhere in New York. So I was, like, I’ll just go around taking little videos, calling these people sheep and ‘baa’-ing at them.”
paragraphs.zh: 正是队伍现象中的这一面——行为是由算法和网红经济驱动的——触碰了 Ezra 的神经。他比起愤怒更是困惑：“这一切开始时只是个玩笑，”他说，“但看到纽约到处都是这些队伍，我真是大开眼界。所以我当时就想，干脆去拍几段小视频，把这些人叫做‘羊’，对着他们‘咩咩’几下。”

paragraphs.en: Ezra stays inconspicuous by filming the lines with a pair of Ray-Ban Meta sunglasses, which synch with his iPhone. A strapping, handsome guy who works in real-estate development, he is hardly the picture of a social-media guerrilla. He realizes that @sheepofnyc has found its audience via the same algorithmic alchemy that drives the fro-yo fervor. One post, filmed by a friend, of an absurdly long line of people waiting to enter the SoHo clothing store New York or Nowhere, has been viewed nearly a million times. It helps that the friend is better at “baa”-ing than Ezra is, emitting a convincing ovine bleat worthy of a flock in County Clare.
paragraphs.zh: Ezra 通过一副与 iPhone 同步的 Ray-Ban Meta 太阳镜来悄无声息地拍摄排队场景。他是一个在房地产开发行业工作的高大、英俊男孩，完全不像社交媒体游击队那样的形象。他也很清楚，账号 @sheepofnyc 之所以吸引到观众，靠的正是与酸奶冰淇淋热潮同样的算法魔法。有一次，一位朋友拍下的一张照片里，一条荒唐到离谱的长队正挤在索霍服装店 New York or Nowhere 的门口，观看次数几乎达到一百万。恰好这位朋友比 Ezra 更会“咩咩”——那种发声很像羊群，会让你想到爱尔兰科威尔县（County Clare）的一大群羊群。

paragraphs.en: Ezra now receives five to ten guest submissions a week. He hasn’t posted many of them, usually because they’re poorly shot, have no audio, or are too mean. He has also received overtures from clothing companies looking to collaborate with @sheepofnyc on T-shirts. One mockup came with M.T.A.-style lettering that reads “SHEEP CENTRAL STATION.” Another bears an image of a sheep Statue of Liberty holding a selfie stick instead of a torch.
paragraphs.zh: Ezra 现在每周会收到五到十条来自观众的投稿。他并没发布太多，大多因为画面拍得太差、没有声音，或者内容太刻薄。他还收到了服装品牌的合作邀约，想和 @sheepofnyc 联名 T 恤。有一家给出的设计稿用了 M.T.A. 风格字体，上面写着“”SHEEP CENTRAL STATION（羊站中央车站）“”。另一款则画了一只拿着自拍杆而不是火炬的“羊版自由女神像”。

paragraphs.en: “I don’t care about monetizing this,” Ezra said, sitting on a bench where some Myka customers were eating their fro-yo. He also doesn’t want to come off as a scold. “A lot of it is tourists, and I feel bad,” he said. “This city is amazing, every part of it. I guess the message is ‘Stop waiting on line.’ Go walk the West Side Highway.” Instead of wasting time on the line for L’industrie pizza on Christopher Street, he suggested, “hop on a subway into real Brooklyn and try the best pizza ever, at Di Fara.” There’s usually a queue there, too, but the Di Fara line is the hard-won result of decades of pizza excellence. The shepherd of @sheepofnyc may be young, but he is wise enough to get that on-line life, like online life, is not really living. ♦
paragraphs.zh: “我并不在乎靠这个挣钱，” Ezra 说着，坐在一张长椅上，周围有一些 Myka 的顾客正在吃他们的 fro-yo。 他也不想显得像个说教的人。“我觉得其中有很大一部分是游客，这让我很难受，”他说。 “这座城市太棒了，每个地方都很棒。我想表达的是：‘别在队列里耗着。去走走西侧公路。’ 他建议，不要在克里斯托弗街（Christopher Street）上的 L’industrie 披萨店排队，而是“坐地铁去真正的布鲁克林，在 Di Fara 吃一顿有史以来最好吃的披萨。” 那里通常也有队伍，但 Di Fara 的队伍是几十年披萨品质沉淀出来的结果。 @sheepofnyc 的“牧羊人”虽然年轻，却足够明白，正如线上的生活一样，线上世界也不是真正的生活。♦
